walkthrough.title: Choose Board Side and Filter Components
walkthrough.text: Let's get Brass: Birmingham set up. First, place the board on the table and pick either the day side or the night side. Both play the same, so choose whichever your group prefers. Next, check your player count. If you have fewer than four players, remove all cards and merchant tiles that show a player count higher than your group from the game and return them to the box.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Wild Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the eight wild cards. There are four wild location cards and four wild industry cards. Separate them from the rest of the deck and place them face up on two of the three card draw areas on the board. These wilds are always available and never get shuffled into the main deck.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Draw Deck
walkthrough.text: Take all the remaining location and industry cards and shuffle them thoroughly. Place them face down on the third card draw area on the board. This is your draw deck for the Canal Era. Remember, in a two-player game the blue and teal location cards are removed, and in a three-player game the teal cards are removed.

walkthrough.title: Place the Merchant Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the remaining merchant tiles after you removed any that exceeded your player count. Shuffle them face down, then place one face up on each merchant space around the edges of the board that matches your player count. In a two-player game, Warrington and Nottingham get no merchants. In a three-player game, Nottingham gets no merchant.

walkthrough.title: Place Merchant Beer
walkthrough.text: Now look at each merchant tile you just placed. Beside each non-blank merchant tile there is a small beer barrel space. Place one beer barrel from the supply onto each of those spaces. These barrels represent the beer available at each merchant for selling goods.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Coal Market
walkthrough.text: Find the black coal cubes and the coal market track on the board. Place one black cube on each space of the coal market, but leave one of the one-pound spaces empty. This means coal starts cheap but gets more expensive as players buy from the market.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Iron Market
walkthrough.text: Now find the orange iron cubes and the iron market track. Place one orange cube on each space of the iron market, but leave both of the one-pound spaces empty. Iron starts slightly more scarce than coal right from the beginning.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the General Supply and Bank
walkthrough.text: Place all remaining coal cubes, iron cubes, and beer barrels beside the board. This is the general supply where consumed resources return to. Place all the money tokens beside the board as well. This pile is the bank.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Mats
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one player mat. This mat has slots for all six types of industry tiles and shows the cost to build each level. Keep it in front of you where you can easily reference the costs throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Take Starting Money and Choose a Color
walkthrough.text: Each player takes seventeen pounds from the bank. Then each player chooses a colored character tile. This tile identifies you and will track your turn order and spending throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Collect Your Link and Industry Tiles
walkthrough.text: Take the fourteen link tiles matching your color and place them in your player area. Then take the forty-five industry tiles matching your color. Stack them on the appropriate slots on your player mat with the black-topped side face down. You should have stacks for cotton mills, coal mines, iron works, manufacturers, potteries, and breweries.

walkthrough.title: Place VP and Income Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player places their victory point marker on the zero space of the progress track around the edge of the board. Then place your income marker on the ten space of the same track. Your income level determines how much money you collect at the end of each round.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player draws eight cards from the draw deck. Keep these hidden from other players. Then draw one additional card and place it face down in your player area. This single card starts your personal discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order and Begin
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all players' character tiles together and place them in a random order on the turn order track. The player whose tile is first will act first this round. Remember, in the very first round of the Canal Era, each player performs only one action instead of the normal two. After that first round, you will always take two actions per turn. Let's learn the rules.

Remove cards and merchant tiles exceeding your player count
2P: remove 3+ and 4+ cards/tiles; 3P: remove 4+ cards/tiles

2P: skip Warrington + Nottingham; 3P: skip Nottingham

summary.title: Determine Turn Order and Begin
bullets: Shuffle all character tiles → random order on turn order track
First Canal Era round: 1 action per player only
All subsequent rounds: 2 actions per turn

walkthrough.title: The Two Eras
walkthrough.text: Brass: Birmingham is played over two eras. The Canal Era represents the years 1770 to 1830 when waterways dominated transport. The Rail Era covers 1830 to 1870 when railways transformed industry. You play the Canal Era first, score it, transition the board, then play the Rail Era and score again.

walkthrough.title: Rounds and Turns
walkthrough.text: Each era is played in rounds. In a four-player game there are eight rounds per era, in a three-player game there are nine, and in a two-player game there are ten. On your turn you perform two actions. The one exception is the very first round of the Canal Era, where each player performs only one action.

walkthrough.title: Card-Driven Actions
walkthrough.text: Every action you take requires discarding one card from your hand face up onto your discard pile. It does not matter which card you discard for most actions. The one exception is the Build action, which requires a specific type of card. After you finish both actions, refill your hand back to eight cards from the draw deck.

walkthrough.title: Spending Money and Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Whenever you spend money on an action, place the spent coins on your character tile on the turn order track instead of returning them to the bank. At the end of the round, the player who spent the least money goes first next round, and the player who spent the most goes last. If players are tied, their relative order stays the same. After reordering, all money on character tiles goes back to the bank.

walkthrough.title: Your Network
walkthrough.text: A location is part of your network if it contains one of your industry tiles or is adjacent to one of your link tiles. Two locations are connected if you can trace a path of link tiles between them, and those links can belong to any player. Your network determines where you can build and where you can source resources.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build an Industry
walkthrough.text: To build, discard a location card to build any industry at that named location, even if it is not in your network. Alternatively, discard an industry card to build that industry type at any location in your network. Take the lowest-level tile of that industry from your player mat, pay its money cost, consume any required coal and iron, then place the tile on an appropriate empty space at that location.

walkthrough.title: Build Restrictions by Era
walkthrough.text: During the Canal Era, you may have only one of your industry tiles per location. During the Rail Era, you can place multiple tiles in the same location. Some tiles on your player mat have a canal-only icon to the left of their slot, meaning they cannot be built during the Rail Era. You must build or develop past them during the Canal Era to access higher tiles.

walkthrough.title: What Happens When You Build Resource Industries
walkthrough.text: When you build a coal mine or iron works, place resource cubes on the tile equal to the number shown in its bottom-right corner. Immediately move as many cubes as possible from the tile into the matching market, filling the most expensive spaces first. For each cube moved, collect the money shown beside that market space. When you build a brewery, place one beer barrel on it during the Canal Era or two during the Rail Era.

walkthrough.title: Flipping Industry Tiles
walkthrough.text: Flipping a tile is how you unlock its victory points and income advancement. Cotton mills, manufacturers, and potteries flip when you perform a Sell action for them. Coal mines, iron works, and breweries flip automatically when the last resource cube or beer barrel is removed from the tile. This can even happen on an opponent's turn when they consume your resources.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build Network
walkthrough.text: To expand your network, discard any card and place a link tile on an undeveloped route adjacent to your network. During the Canal Era, you place one canal link for three pounds with no resource cost. During the Rail Era, you can place one rail link for five pounds plus one coal, or two rail links for fifteen pounds plus two coal plus one beer. Each link must connect to your network and a coal source after placement.

walkthrough.title: Action: Develop
walkthrough.text: To develop, discard any card and remove one or two of your lowest-level industry tiles from your player mat. Return them to the box. For each tile removed, consume one iron. Developing lets you skip weak early tiles to access powerful higher-level tiles sooner. You cannot develop pottery tiles that show the lightbulb icon. Those must be built on the board to clear them.

walkthrough.title: Action: Sell
walkthrough.text: To sell, discard any card and choose one of your unflipped cotton mills, manufacturers, or potteries that is connected to a merchant tile showing that industry's icon. Consume the required amount of beer shown in the top-right corner of the industry tile. Then flip the tile and advance your income marker. You can repeat the sell process for additional tiles in the same action.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Beer Bonuses
walkthrough.text: When selling, if you use the beer barrel beside the merchant tile you are selling to, you also receive that merchant's bonus. Gloucester gives a free develop. Oxford advances your income by two spaces. Nottingham and Shrewsbury give bonus victory points. Warrington gives five pounds from the bank.

walkthrough.title: Action: Loan
walkthrough.text: To take a loan, discard any card and take thirty pounds from the bank. Then move your income marker backward three income levels on the progress track. Loans are never repaid, but they permanently reduce your income. You cannot take a loan if it would drop your income level below negative ten.

walkthrough.title: Action: Scout
walkthrough.text: To scout, discard three cards total from your hand. In return, take one wild location card and one wild industry card. You are trading three cards for two flexible wilds. You cannot scout if you already have a wild card in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Consuming Coal
walkthrough.text: When an action requires coal, you must take it from the closest connected unflipped coal mine belonging to any player, which is free. If you are not connected to any coal mine, you may purchase coal from the coal market at the current price, but this requires a connection to a merchant space. If the market is empty, coal costs eight pounds each.

walkthrough.title: Consuming Iron
walkthrough.text: Iron works differently from coal because it does not require a connection. Take iron from any unflipped iron works on the board belonging to any player, which is free. If no unflipped iron works exist anywhere on the board, buy iron from the iron market at the current price. If the market is empty, iron costs six pounds each.

walkthrough.title: Consuming Beer
walkthrough.text: Beer comes from three sources. Your own unflipped breweries can supply beer without needing a connection. An opponent's unflipped brewery can supply beer but must be connected to the location where the beer is needed. Merchant beer beside a merchant tile can only be consumed as part of a Sell action to that merchant.

walkthrough.title: Overbuilding
walkthrough.text: Sometimes you can replace an already-placed industry tile with a higher-level tile of the same type. You can overbuild any of your own tiles at any time, paying normal build costs. You can overbuild an opponent's coal mine or iron works, but only when there are zero resource cubes of that type on any tile on the entire board and in the market. Overbuilt tiles are removed from the game entirely.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken their turns, rearrange the character tiles on the turn order track based on who spent the least money. Then return all money from the character tiles to the bank. Finally, each player collects income from the bank equal to their income level on the progress track. If your income is negative, you must pay that amount to the bank instead.

walkthrough.title: Era Scoring
walkthrough.text: At the end of each era, scoring happens in two steps. First, score your link tiles. For each of your links, count the number of industry icons and beer barrel icons displayed in the two adjacent locations and score one victory point per icon. Remove your links from the board after scoring. Second, score all your flipped industry tiles by their printed VP value. Unflipped tiles score nothing.

walkthrough.title: Canal-to-Rail Transition
walkthrough.text: After Canal Era scoring, several cleanup steps happen. Remove all canal link tiles from the board. Remove all level one industry tiles from the board and return them to the box. Level two and higher tiles stay on the board. Reset merchant beer by placing one barrel on each empty merchant beer space. Finally, shuffle all discard piles together to form a new draw deck for the Rail Era.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends after Rail Era scoring is complete. The player with the most victory points wins. If there is a tie, the player with the higher income level wins. If still tied, the player with the most remaining money wins. If still tied, those players share the victory.

bullets: Score at end of each era, VP accumulated across both
Winner = most VP after Rail Era scoring

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Birmingham
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through scripted turns in the Canal Era with three example players. Each player will demonstrate different strategies and mistakes so you can see how the game's systems interact. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: The Canal Era Opens
walkthrough.text: Remember, in the very first round of the Canal Era, each player takes only one action instead of two. This means your opening move is especially important. Think of it as planting a seed for your entire Canal Era strategy. Each player has eight cards and seventeen pounds to start.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Build a Coal Mine
walkthrough.text: They pay the money cost from their starting cash and place coal cubes on the tile. Some cubes immediately move to the coal market, earning Player One a few pounds back. This is a strong opening because it creates a coal source other players may need.

walkthrough.title: Why Coal First Is Smart
walkthrough.text: Coal mines are powerful for two reasons. First, selling cubes to the market gives you immediate cash flow. Second, when opponents consume coal from your mine, those cubes leave the tile, and when the last cube is removed, the tile flips automatically. That means your opponents are helping you score points and gain income without you spending an action.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Build a Cotton Mill
walkthrough.text: They pay the money cost but no resources are needed for this tile. The cotton mill sits on the board unflipped. To flip it and score points, Player Two will eventually need to perform a Sell action, which requires a connection to a merchant with a cotton icon and enough beer.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Build a Canal Link
walkthrough.text: This expands their network so they can build in more places next round. During the Canal Era, canal links cost only money with no resource requirements, making them an affordable way to spread out early.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Begins
walkthrough.text: Starting from round two, each player now takes two actions per turn. Player One takes the first turn this round because they spent the least money in round one. Remember, all spent money goes onto your character tile, and the lowest spender goes first next round. Going first lets you claim contested build locations and scarce resources before others.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Build and Develop
walkthrough.text: For their second action, they develop. They remove a low-level industry tile from their player mat and consume one iron from their own iron works. That iron was free because it came from the board, not the market.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Building Without a Flip Path
walkthrough.text: Player Two uses both actions to build two more cotton mills in different locations. They now have three cotton mills on the board, but none of them are connected to a merchant with a cotton demand icon. Without that connection, Player Two cannot perform a Sell action to flip any of these tiles. They have spent most of their money building industries that cannot score.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Plan Your Flip
walkthrough.text: Before you build any cotton mill, manufacturer, or pottery, ask yourself how you will flip it. These tiles only flip through the Sell action, which requires a connection to a matching merchant and enough beer. If you build tiles you cannot sell, they sit on the board as dead weight scoring zero points at era end. Always plan your flip path before you spend money building.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Take a Loan
walkthrough.text: Player Three realizes they need more money to execute their plan. They discard a card and take a thirty-pound loan from the bank. Their income marker drops three income levels. This is a good decision because the cash allows two strong plays next turn. Do not be afraid of loans. Income does not directly score victory points, and experienced players often take early loans.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Beer
walkthrough.text: Player Two finally builds a canal link to reach a merchant with a cotton icon. Excited, they try to sell a cotton mill. But the tile requires one beer to sell, and Player Two has no brewery on the board, is not connected to anyone else's brewery, and the merchant's beer barrel is already gone. The sell action is illegal. Player Two wasted their entire turn getting into position for a sale they cannot complete.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Secure Beer Before You Need It
walkthrough.text: Beer is the gatekeeper for selling goods and building rail links. If you plan to sell cotton, manufactured goods, or pottery, you need a beer source. Build a brewery early so you always have access. Your own breweries do not even need to be connected to where the beer is consumed. Ignoring beer until you need it often means wasting a full turn scrambling to get it.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Strategic Consumption
walkthrough.text: Player One builds a brewery near one of their coal mines. Now they have a self-contained supply chain: coal for building, iron for developing, and beer for selling. Notice how Player One's tiles work together. Their coal mine may flip soon because other players keep consuming coal from it. Every resource taken from your mines helps you score.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Not Developing Past Weak Tiles
walkthrough.text: Player Three built a level one manufacturer early in the game and now wants to build a level two. But the level two manufacturer requires coal, and Player Three is not connected to any coal source. They also have not developed past their weakest tiles in other industries. Their player mat is clogged with low-level tiles they will never want to build, and they have no clear path to stronger ones.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Develop Early and Often
walkthrough.text: The Develop action lets you skip weak tiles to access powerful higher-level ones. Do not try to build every tile on your player mat. Identify which industries you want to focus on and develop past the rest. One iron per tile developed is a small price to unlock tiles worth significantly more points and income. Develop during the Canal Era to position yourself for a strong Rail Era.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Building Links Without Purpose
walkthrough.text: Player Three builds two canal links reaching into a remote corner of the board with no merchants and no good building spaces. These links cost six pounds and two actions but do not connect Player Three to any useful resources, merchants, or high-value locations. At era scoring, each link scores one VP per adjacent industry icon, and this remote area has very few.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Every Link Should Serve a Purpose
walkthrough.text: Links score based on the industry icons adjacent to them. Build links toward areas with many industry spaces and merchant connections, not toward empty corners. Every link should either enable a future build, connect you to a resource, or sit between locations packed with industry icons for maximum scoring. A three-pound canal link to nowhere is three pounds wasted.

walkthrough.title: Canal Era Scoring
walkthrough.text: The Canal Era ends when all cards are exhausted. Now we score. Player One has several flipped tiles from opponents consuming their resources and from smart sell actions. Their links sit between busy locations earning solid VP. Player Two has three unflipped cotton mills scoring nothing. Player Three has links in poor locations earning minimal points. Player One leads by a wide margin.

walkthrough.title: Canal-to-Rail Transition
walkthrough.text: After scoring, remove all canal links from the board. Remove all level one industry tiles from the board. Any level two or higher tiles remain and can score again at the end of the Rail Era. Reset the merchant beer barrels and shuffle all discard piles into a new draw deck. The Rail Era begins with a fresh hand of eight cards each.

walkthrough.title: Rail Era Differences
walkthrough.text: The Rail Era plays similarly but with key differences. Rail links cost five pounds each plus one coal, and building two links in one action also costs a beer. You can now build multiple tiles in the same location. The stakes are higher because tiles score more VP and the game ends after this era. Everything you build now is your final scoring push.

walkthrough.title: Putting It All Together
walkthrough.text: Player Two has learned from their Canal Era mistakes. This time they build a brewery first, develop past weak tiles to reach level three cotton mills, and plan a sell route to a merchant before building. They flip three tiles in the Rail Era and climb back into contention. Player Three focuses their links on high-density areas and finishes with respectable link scoring.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how coal mines flip through consumption, why beer access is critical for selling, why developing past weak tiles matters, and how link placement affects scoring. Remember these lessons and you will avoid the most common beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first full game, here are three resources to help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy fundamentals and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of all industry types, actions, and resources.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck, industrialist!

Cost: £6 + 2 actions for minimal era scoring
Links in empty areas score very few VP

summary.title: Lesson: Every Link Should Serve a Purpose
bullets: Links score 1 VP per adjacent industry icon
Build toward high-density areas and merchant connections
Every link should enable builds, connect resources, or maximize scoring

summary.title: Canal Era Scoring
bullets: Player One leads: flipped tiles + well-placed links
Player Two: 3 unflipped cotton mills = 0 VP
Player Three: poor link placement = minimal scoring

summary.title: Canal-to-Rail Transition
bullets: Remove all canal links + all level 1 tiles from board
Level 2+ tiles remain and can score again in Rail Era
Reset merchant beer; shuffle discards into new draw deck

summary.title: Rail Era Differences
bullets: Rail links: £5 + 1 coal each; 2 links also cost 1 beer
Multiple tiles per location allowed
Higher-level tiles available; final scoring push

summary.title: Putting It All Together
bullets: Player Two learns from mistakes: brewery first, develop, plan sell route
Player Three focuses links on high-density areas
Applied lessons lead to competitive Rail Era performance

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Covered coal flipping via consumption, beer for selling, developing past weak tiles, link scoring
Biggest traps: building without flip path, ignoring beer, skipping develop, aimless links

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy fundamentals
Reference the Appendix → quick definitions for all terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up new players the most. Building industries with no path to flip them is the biggest one. Taking too many loans without a plan to capitalize on the cash is another. Ignoring the Develop action and being stuck with weak tiles deep into the game will cripple your scoring potential. Finally, building links that do not connect you to merchants or high-value locations wastes actions and money.

walkthrough.title: Cash Flow Is King
walkthrough.text: Money drives everything in Brass: Birmingham. You need it to build industries, place links, and consume resources from the market. Do not be afraid to take loans early. A well-timed thirty-pound loan in round two can fund a powerful canal era that more than compensates for the income loss. The key is spending that loan money on actions that flip tiles and generate ongoing income. An early loan that funds two flipped tiles is far better than hoarding cash and building nothing.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Flip Path
walkthrough.text: Every industry tile you build should have a clear path to being flipped before you spend the money. For cotton mills, manufacturers, and potteries, that means knowing which merchant you will sell to, having or planning a connection to that merchant, and having beer available. For coal mines and iron works, that means building them where other players will want to consume from them, or where you will consume the resources yourself for future builds. If you cannot see how a tile flips, do not build it yet.

walkthrough.title: Beer Is the Bottleneck
walkthrough.text: Beer is required for selling goods and for building two rail links in one action. New players consistently underestimate how important beer access is. Build at least one brewery early in each era so you always have a supply. Your own breweries do not need a connection to where the beer is consumed. If you rely on opponent breweries, you need a connection, and they might flip before you use them. Controlling your own beer supply gives you freedom to sell whenever the timing is right.

walkthrough.title: Develop to Win
walkthrough.text: The Develop action is one of the most underused actions among beginners. Removing a weak level one tile and paying one iron to unlock a level three or four tile is an incredible deal. Higher-level tiles score more VP, generate more income, and produce more resources. During the Canal Era, try to develop past canal-only tiles so you enter the Rail Era with only strong options on your player mat. One develop action early can save you two wasted builds later.

walkthrough.title: Make Your Opponents Help You
walkthrough.text: One of the most elegant aspects of Brass is that opponents consuming your resources actually helps you. When someone takes coal from your mine or iron from your works, those cubes leave the tile. When the last one leaves, the tile flips for free, giving you VP and income without spending an action. Build resource industries in locations where other players have high demand. Position your coal mines where rail builders need coal and your iron works where developers need iron.

walkthrough.title: Think Two Eras Ahead
walkthrough.text: Level two and higher industry tiles survive the Canal-to-Rail transition. A flipped level three cotton mill built during the Canal Era will score its VP again at the end of the Rail Era. That is double scoring from a single build. Prioritize developing past level one tiles and building level two or higher industries during the Canal Era whenever possible. The transition rewards players who think beyond the immediate era.

walkthrough.title: Link Placement for Maximum Scoring
walkthrough.text: Links score one VP per industry icon and beer barrel icon displayed in their two adjacent locations. The most valuable link positions are between two locations that each have multiple industry spaces. A single link between two four-icon locations scores eight VP. Plan your network routes through the densest parts of the board. In the Rail Era especially, high-scoring links can be worth fifteen to twenty VP each when adjacent to fully developed locations.

bullets: Building industries with no flip path = dead tiles scoring 0
Too many loans without capitalizing on the cash
Ignoring Develop → stuck with weak tiles all game
Aimless links that don't connect to merchants or scoring positions

Income level ≠ VP; don't fear low income if you're scoring
Hoarding cash and building nothing is worse than low income

If you can't see how it flips, don't build it yet
Every tile should have a scoring plan before you spend £

summary.title: Make Your Opponents Help You

Free flips = VP + income without spending an action

summary.title: Think Two Eras Ahead

Flipped level 3+ tiles score VP in BOTH eras = double value
Prioritize building level 2+ during Canal Era

summary.title: Link Placement for Maximum Scoring
bullets: Links score 1 VP per adjacent industry/beer icon
Best positions: between two high-density locations
Plan network routes through busiest board areas
Rail Era links can score 15-20 VP each in dense areas

walkthrough.title: The Coal Baron Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest Canal Era openings is building two coal mines early in high-traffic locations. Other players need coal for rail links and certain industries, so they consume from your mines. Each consumption brings your mines closer to flipping for free. Meanwhile, you sell excess cubes to the market for immediate cash. By mid-Canal Era, you can have multiple flipped mines scoring VP and boosting income while your opponents did the work for you.

walkthrough.title: The Iron Development Engine
walkthrough.text: Iron works combined with aggressive developing creates a self-reinforcing engine. Build one or two iron works early, then use the Develop action repeatedly. Each develop consumes iron from your own works for free, stripping cubes from them. When the last cube leaves, the iron works flips, giving you VP and income. Meanwhile, you have skipped past weak tiles on your player mat and now have access to high-level industries that score massive points.

walkthrough.title: The Beer Monopoly
walkthrough.text: Beer is the scarcest resource in Brass: Birmingham. Building three or four breweries early creates a stranglehold on selling. Other players who need beer to sell their goods must connect to your breweries, and when they consume from you, your breweries flip. Merchant beer is limited and does not regenerate until the Canal-to-Rail transition. Controlling beer access means you can sell whenever you want while opponents wait for their turn.

walkthrough.title: The Double-Score Development Rush
walkthrough.text: Level two and higher tiles survive the Canal-to-Rail transition and score their VP again. The most point-efficient strategy is developing to level three or four during the Canal Era, building and flipping those tiles, then scoring them twice. A flipped level three cotton mill scores nine VP per era for a total of eighteen VP from one tile. Use the Develop action aggressively in the first three rounds to reach these tiles early.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Turn order in Brass is determined by who spent the least money. Going first lets you claim contested locations, grab scarce resources, and sell before merchants run out of beer. Intentionally spending less in one round to secure first position next round can be worth more than the action you sacrificed. Conversely, going last is fine when the board state does not threaten your plans. Master turn order manipulation and you control the tempo of the game.

walkthrough.title: Rail Era Link Scoring Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the Rail Era, link scoring is where games are won or lost. A single rail link between two fully developed locations can score eight to twelve VP. Two links in a high-density corridor can score twenty or more. Plan your rail network to pass through the most industrialized parts of the board. Build industries in locations specifically to increase the icon count for your adjacent links. The best players treat link placement as their primary scoring mechanism.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Beer Bonus Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Each merchant offers a unique bonus when you consume their beer barrel during a sell action. The Gloucester develop bonus is worth the price of a full action. The Nottingham and Shrewsbury VP bonuses directly increase your score. Plan your sell routes to hit merchants whose bonuses align with your strategy. In a tight game, collecting two or three merchant bonuses over the course of an era can be the margin of victory.

walkthrough.title: The Pottery Wildcard
walkthrough.text: Potteries are unique because their level one tile can be built during the Rail Era, unlike other level one tiles. Higher-level potteries score enormous VP but require significant beer. The lightbulb icon on certain pottery tiles prevents developing past them, so you must build them on the board to clear the way. Smart players identify early whether the pottery path is viable by checking their card distribution, merchant availability, and beer access. When it works, pottery can dominate the scoreboard.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board and Adapting
walkthrough.text: No strategy survives first contact with three opponents. The best Brass players read the board constantly and adapt. If two opponents are fighting over cotton merchants, pivot to manufacturers or potteries. If coal mines are plentiful, skip building your own and consume from others. If beer is scarce, build breweries to exploit demand. Flexibility beats rigidity. Keep two or three viable paths in mind and commit to the one the board state rewards.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Tempo and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: In the final two or three rounds of the Rail Era, switch entirely to scoring mode. Stop building anything that cannot flip before the era ends. Focus on selling remaining goods, placing links in maximum-value positions, and consuming resources to flip your last tiles. Count your opponents' VP and identify whether you need to play safe or take risks. The player who transitions from building to scoring at the right moment wins the tightest games.

entries.definition: The first era of the game, representing 1770-1830. Players build canal links and industries. At era end, all canal links and level one industry tiles are removed from the board. Level two and higher tiles remain for the Rail Era.

The second and final era of the game, representing 1830-1870. Players build rail links and higher-level industries. The game ends after Rail Era scoring. Rail links require coal and optionally beer to place.

The cleanup phase between eras. Remove all canal links and level one tiles from the board. Reset merchant beer barrels. Shuffle all discard piles into a new draw deck. Level two and higher tiles remain on the board and can score again.

Place an industry tile from your player mat onto the board at a valid location. Requires discarding a location card (build at named location, no network needed) or an industry card (build matching type in your network). Pay money cost plus any required coal and iron.

Place link tiles to expand your network. Canal Era: one canal link for three pounds. Rail Era: one rail link for five pounds plus one coal, or two links for fifteen pounds plus two coal plus one beer. Links must connect to your existing network.

Flip unflipped cotton mills, manufacturers, or potteries by selling to a connected merchant with a matching demand icon. Consume required beer. May repeat for additional tiles in the same action. Advances your income marker.

Remove one or two of your lowest-level industry tiles from your player mat and return them to the box. Consume one iron per tile removed. Skips weak tiles to access higher-level industries sooner. Cannot develop pottery tiles with the lightbulb icon.

Take thirty pounds from the bank. Move your income marker backward three income levels on the progress track. Loans are never repaid. Cannot loan if it would drop income below level negative ten. Requires discarding a card.
Discard three cards total from your hand. Take one wild location card and one wild industry card. Cannot scout if you already hold a wild card. Trades three cards for two flexible wilds.
Instead of performing an action, you may pass, but you must still discard a card for each passed action. Passing is sometimes useful to save money and improve turn order for the next round.

An industry tile that produces cotton goods. Flipped via the Sell action by connecting to a merchant with a cotton demand icon and consuming required beer. Scores VP at era end when flipped. Available in multiple levels with increasing VP and income.

An industry tile that produces coal cubes. When built, place cubes on the tile and sell excess to the coal market for money. Flips automatically when the last cube is removed. Other players consuming your coal helps flip your mine.

An industry tile that produces iron cubes. When built, place cubes on the tile and sell excess to the iron market for money. Flips automatically when the last cube is removed. Iron can be consumed from any iron works on the board without needing a connection.

An industry tile that produces manufactured goods. Flipped via the Sell action by connecting to a merchant with a manufactured goods demand icon and consuming required beer. Higher levels score significant VP.

An industry tile that produces pottery. Unique because level one pottery can be built during the Rail Era. Some pottery tiles have a lightbulb icon preventing development. Flipped via the Sell action. High-level potteries score among the most VP in the game.

An industry tile that produces beer barrels. Receives one barrel during Canal Era builds or two during Rail Era builds. Flips when its last barrel is removed. Your own breweries supply beer without needing a connection. Opponent breweries require a connection.

A black cube resource required to build rail links and certain industry tiles. Must be sourced from the closest connected coal mine (free) or purchased from the coal market (requires merchant connection). Consumed cubes return to the general supply.

An orange cube resource required for the Develop action and building certain industry tiles. Can be sourced from any unflipped iron works on the board regardless of connection (free) or purchased from the iron market. No connection needed for iron consumption.

A barrel resource required for selling cotton, manufactured goods, and pottery, and for building two rail links in one action. Sources: your own breweries (no connection needed), opponent breweries (connection required), or merchant beer (Sell action only).

The primary currency used to build industries, place links, and take loans. Starting amount is seventeen pounds. Spent money goes onto your character tile on the turn order track, determining next-round order. Collected as income at end of each round.

A market track on the board where coal cubes can be purchased when no connected coal mine is available. Starts nearly full with one of the one-pound spaces empty. Prices increase as cubes are bought. If empty, coal costs eight pounds each. Requires a connection to a merchant space to buy.

A market track on the board where iron cubes can be purchased when no unflipped iron works exists. Starts with both one-pound spaces empty. Prices increase as cubes are bought. If empty, iron costs six pounds each. No connection required to buy from the iron market.

Turning an industry tile to its black-topped side. Flipped tiles score VP at era end and advance your income marker. Cotton mills, manufacturers, and potteries flip via the Sell action. Coal mines, iron works, and breweries flip when their last resource is removed.

The set of locations you can access for building. A location is in your network if it contains one of your industry tiles or is adjacent to one of your link tiles. Building with a location card bypasses the network requirement.

Two locations are connected if you can trace a continuous path of link tiles between them, regardless of who owns those links. Connection is required for consuming coal from mines, consuming beer from opponent breweries, and selling to merchants.

Determined each round by money spent. The player who spent the least goes first next round. All spent money sits on character tiles until end of round. Going first allows claiming contested locations and resources. Ties preserve existing relative order.

Tracked on the progress track. Players collect money equal to their income level at the end of each round, except the final round. Income is increased when tiles are flipped. Income can be negative, requiring payment to the bank. Income level is the first tie-breaker at game end.

Replacing an already-placed industry tile with a higher-level tile of the same type. You can overbuild your own tiles freely. You can overbuild an opponent's coal mine or iron works only when zero cubes of that resource type exist on the board and in the market. Overbuilt tiles are removed from the game.

A personal board with slots for all six industry types. Shows the money cost, coal cost, and iron cost for each tile level. Tiles are stacked in order, and you must build or develop the lowest-level tile first. Canal-only tiles are marked with an icon to the left of their slot.

A tile representing your player on the turn order track. Spent money is placed on this tile during your turn. At end of round, characters are reordered by money spent (least first) and money returns to the bank.

A tile placed on routes between locations to expand your network. Canal links are used during the Canal Era and rail links during the Rail Era. Links score VP at era end based on adjacent industry icons. Each player has fourteen link tiles.

Tiles placed around the board edges representing external markets. Each merchant shows demand icons for specific industry types. Selling requires connection to a matching merchant. Each merchant has a beer barrel space and a unique bonus when that beer is consumed.

A card showing a specific location name. When discarded for the Build action, allows building any industry at that location even if it is not in your network. For all other actions, can be discarded as any card.
A card showing an industry type icon. When discarded for the Build action, allows building that industry type at any location in your network. For all other actions, can be discarded as any card.

A special card that can be played as any location card for the Build action. When discarded, it returns to its card draw area instead of going to your discard pile. Cannot be used to build at farm brewery locations. Four wild location cards exist in the game.

A special card that can be played as any industry card for the Build action. When discarded, it returns to its card draw area instead of going to your discard pile. Four wild industry cards exist in the game.

The track around the board edge used to track both VP and income. VP markers start at zero and advance through scoring. Income markers start at ten and move based on tile flips and loans. If VP exceeds one hundred, loop the track again.
The pool of coal cubes, iron cubes, and beer barrels kept beside the board. Consumed resources return here. Resources placed on newly built tiles come from here. Separate from the market tracks.

Two unnamed locations on the board, each with one brewery-only space. Can only be built using a brewery industry card or wild industry card, not a wild location card. One farm brewery requires a link from Cannock; the other connects automatically between Kidderminster and Worcester.

An icon on certain pottery tiles that prevents them from being developed. These tiles must be removed from the player mat by building them on the board. They block access to higher-level pottery tiles until cleared.

Industry tiles marked with a special icon to the left of their slot on the player mat. These cannot be built during the Rail Era. They must be built or developed away during the Canal Era to access higher-level tiles behind them.

When your income marker falls below zero on the progress track, you must pay that amount to the bank at end of round instead of collecting money. If you cannot pay, you may sell industry tiles from the board at half cost. If still short, lose one VP per pound owed.

At era end, each of your link tiles scores one VP per industry icon and beer barrel icon displayed in the two adjacent locations. Links are removed from the board after scoring. High-density link placement is a primary source of VP.

At era end, each of your flipped industry tiles scores the VP value printed in its bottom-left corner. Unflipped tiles score nothing. Level two and higher tiles that survive the Canal-to-Rail transition can score in both eras.

The measure used to determine the winner. Accumulated from link scoring and industry scoring at the end of each era, plus merchant beer bonuses during play. Tracked on the progress track. Highest VP after Rail Era wins.

A unique reward received when consuming the beer barrel beside a merchant tile during a Sell action. Bonuses vary by merchant: Gloucester gives a free develop, Oxford gives income, Nottingham and Shrewsbury give VP, and Warrington gives money.

Level two and higher industry tiles remain on the board through the Canal-to-Rail transition. If flipped during the Canal Era, they score VP at both Canal and Rail era scoring. This makes early development to higher tile levels extremely valuable.
